title: The Global Grapevine: Why Rumors of Terrorism, Immigration, and Trade Matter By Gary Alan Fine and Bill Ellis Oxford University Press. 2010. 255 pages. $27.50 cloth
abstract: Rumors, contemporary legends and conspiracy theories – long marginalized by academia – are slowly coming in from the cold. Sociologist Gary Alan Fine collaborates here with American Studies scholar and folklorist Bill Ellis to examine how “rumor” (a term they use to include all three phenomena) interacts with the current dynamics of increased globalism and declining American power and prestige, and to argue that rumor deserves more attention. (The “global” in The Global Grapevine means aspects of international commerce, politics, immigration, travel and communication, mostly from an American perspective.) They argue that as part of everyday life, rumor has become too easy to discount as an unfounded set of entertaining (if sometimes horrifying) claims or stories. But rumor can matter in very concrete ways: people have been mobbed and killed because of rumors and contemporary legends – from 12th century Britain and 19th century China to Guatemala in the 1990s. Rumor's everyday quality is valuable though, because knowing how rumor works provides insight into hidden trends and divisions in society, and Fine and Ellis hope this can ameliorate some pernicious social effects of globalization.
